feed.title: Wasil Zafar — Technology
feed.subtitle: Latest technology articles from Wasil Zafar

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 28: Emerging ARMv9 & Future Directions
entry.summary: ARMv9.0 (2021) was the first major architectural version since ARMv8 in 2011. It mandated SVE2 for server-class cores, introduced the Realm Management Extension for confidential computing, and continued a decade-long trend of adding new feature flags instead of breaking instruction semantics. This

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 27: Security Research & Exploitation on ARM64
entry.summary: Security research on ARM64 blends intimate knowledge of the ISA with an understanding of OS memory layout, compiler mitigations, and hardware defences. This part covers the attack path from memory disclosure through ROP/JOP chain execution, examines kernel exploitation patterns specific to AArch64,

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 25: Cross-Compilation & Build Systems
entry.summary: Cross-compiling for ARM64 on an x86 host seems straightforward until ABI mismatches, missing sysroots, and LLVM triple confusion bite you. This part covers correct toolchain selection for both bare-metal and Linux targets, CMake toolchain files, LLVM/Clang cross setup, and automated firmware

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 26: ARM in Real Systems — Android, RTOS & Bootloaders
entry.summary: Theory is validated in production. This part walks through five real deployment environments where ARM assembly knowledge is directly exercised: Android native code via the NDK, two embedded RTOSes (FreeRTOS and Zephyr), the U-Boot bootloader that brings up hundreds of millions of Linux boards, and

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 24: Linkers, Loaders & Binary Format Internals
entry.summary: Every ARM binary you run passed through a linker that stitched object files together, assigned addresses, and emitted relocations that the dynamic loader resolves at runtime. This part walks from raw ELF sections through RELA relocation entries, PLT/GOT lazy binding, position-independent code on

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 23: Debugging & Tooling Ecosystem
entry.summary: The ARM debugging ecosystem spans from QEMU software simulation up to hardware probes with instruction-level trace. This part covers the GDB remote serial protocol, OpenOCD configuration for JTAG and SWD adapters, ETM/ITM trace decoding, and practical kernel debugging workflows with QEMU. # ──

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 22: Virtualization Extensions
entry.summary: ARM's Virtualization Extensions give EL2 a hardware-enforced isolation boundary between the hypervisor and every guest OS. This part walks the full path from EL3 dropping into EL2, configuring trap controls, programming two-stage page tables, wiring the virtual GIC, and understanding how KVM uses

entry.title: Generative Model Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: The fundamental problem: we want to maximize $\log p_\theta(x)$ (log-likelihood of observed data), but computing $p_\theta(x) = \int p_\theta(x|z)p(z)\,dz$ is intractable when the latent space is high-dimensional. The solution: introduce an approximate posterior $q_\phi(z|x)$ and derive a tractable

entry.title: Reinforcement Learning Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: Reinforcement learning operates in a Markov Decision Process (MDP) — a mathematical framework for sequential decision-making where outcomes are partly random and partly under the control of a decision-maker (agent). An MDP is a tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$ where: The Markov

entry.title: Contrastive & Metric Learning Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: The goal of metric learning is to learn an embedding function $f_\theta: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}^d$ such that similar inputs map to nearby points and dissimilar inputs map to distant points in the embedding space. Why normalize? With L2-normalized embeddings ($\|u\| = \|v\| = 1$), cosine

entry.title: Explainability & Attribution Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: Given a model $f: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}$ and an input $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$ with prediction $f(x)$, the attribution problem asks: how much did each feature $x_i$ contribute to the output? We want an attribution vector $\phi = (\phi_1, \ldots, \phi_d)$ satisfying desirable properties.

entry.title: NLP Smoothing Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: An n-gram language model estimates $P(w_i | w_{i-n+1}, \ldots, w_{i-1})$ from corpus counts. The maximum likelihood estimate (MLE) is: where $C(\cdot)$ denotes the count. Problem: Any bigram not observed in training gets $P = 0$. Since perplexity involves $\prod P(w_i|w_{i-1})$, a single zero term

entry.title: Detection & Localization Geometry | Math for AI
entry.summary: Given two axis-aligned bounding boxes $B^p = (x_1^p, y_1^p, x_2^p, y_2^p)$ (predicted) and $B^{gt} = (x_1^{gt}, y_1^{gt}, x_2^{gt}, y_2^{gt})$ (ground truth), their Intersection over Union is: Geometric computation: The intersection rectangle has corners at: Intersection area: $|B^p \cap B^{gt}| =

entry.title: Scaling Laws & Compound Optimization | Math for AI
entry.summary: Empirically, neural network test loss follows a power law in three independent variables: where $N$ = parameters, $D$ = training tokens, $C$ = compute (FLOPs), and $\alpha_N, \alpha_D, \alpha_C$ are scaling exponents. On a log-log plot, these appear as straight lines with slope $-\alpha$. The

entry.title: Recommendation & Ranking Mathematics | Math for AI
entry.summary: A user-item interaction matrix $R \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ (m users, n items) is typically sparse — most entries are unobserved. Matrix factorization approximates $R$ as a product of low-rank factors: where $k \ll \min(m,n)$ is the embedding dimension. Each user $u$ gets a vector $\mathbf{u}_i

entry.title: Bayes Classifiers & Naive Bayes in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: At the heart of Bayesian classification lies a simple but profound equation that inverts the direction of probabilistic reasoning. Instead of asking “what data would we see given a class?” (likelihood), we ask “what class is most probable given the observed data?” (posterior). $$P(C_k | x) =

entry.title: Decision Trees from Scratch in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: A decision tree learns a hierarchy of if-then rules that recursively partition the feature space. At each internal node, it asks a question about one feature (“Is $x_i \leq t$?”) and routes the data left or right. Leaf nodes contain the final class predictions. flowchart TD A["x₁ ≤ 3.5?"] -->|Yes|

entry.title: K-Nearest Neighbors in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: K-Nearest Neighbors is one of the simplest yet most powerful classification algorithms. The core idea is beautifully intuitive: to classify a new point, look at the K closest points in the training set and let them vote . No training phase, no learned parameters — just raw geometry in feature space.

entry.title: The Perceptron: Bridge to Deep Learning | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: The perceptron is the ancestor of every neural network in existence today. Introduced by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958, it was inspired by Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts’ 1943 mathematical model of a biological neuron. Understanding the perceptron — especially its famous failure — explains why deep

entry.title: Scikit-learn ↔ PyTorch Interoperability | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Scikit-learn operates on NumPy arrays; PyTorch on tensors. Seamless conversion between them is the foundation of any hybrid workflow. PyTorch and NumPy share the same memory on CPU, enabling near-zero-cost conversion. import numpy as np import torch # NumPy -> PyTorch (shares memory on CPU) arr =

entry.title: ML Workflow: Cross-Validation & Feature Engineering | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: The most fundamental principle in ML evaluation: never evaluate on data used for training . A proper three-way split ensures that your final test accuracy reflects true generalization performance. import torch def train_val_test_split(X, y, train_frac=0.7, val_frac=0.15, seed=42): """Split dataset

entry.title: Mathematical Foundations for PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Probability theory gives us the language to talk about uncertainty. In deep learning it underpins maximum likelihood estimation, Bayesian networks, variational autoencoders, and every stochastic element of training. import torch from torch.distributions import ( Normal, Bernoulli, Categorical,

entry.title: Actor-Critic Methods (A2C) in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: A2C improves over REINFORCE in three ways: $$\mathcal{L} = \underbrace{-\sum_t \log \pi_\theta(a_t|s_t) \hat{A}_t}_{\text{policy loss}} + \underbrace{c_1 \sum_t (V_\phi(s_t) - G_t)^2}_{\text{value loss}} - \underbrace{c_2 H[\pi_\theta(\cdot|s_t)]}_{\text{entropy bonus}}$$ import torch import

entry.title: Deep Q-Network (DQN) in PyTorch from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: DQN (Mnih et al., 2013) solved the instability of using neural networks as Q-function approximators with three innovations: import torch from collections import deque import random class ReplayBuffer: """ Circular buffer storing (s, a, r, s', done) transitions. Random sampling breaks temporal

entry.title: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: An MDP formally defines the RL problem as a tuple $(S, A, P, R, \gamma)$: import torch class GridWorld: """ Simple deterministic GridWorld MDP. States: (row, col) flattened to integer. Actions: 0=UP, 1=RIGHT, 2=DOWN, 3=LEFT. Goal: reach bottom-right corner. """ def __init__(self, rows=4, cols=4):

entry.title: Policy Gradient Methods & REINFORCE in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: The policy gradient theorem gives us an unbiased gradient estimator for the expected return $J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[G_0]$: $$\nabla_\theta J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_\tau\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(A_t|S_t) \cdot G_t\right]$$ where $G_t = \sum_{k=t}^{T}

entry.title: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: PPO's core insight: reuse the same rollout data for multiple gradient updates (unlike A2C which uses it once). The danger is that repeated updates on stale data cause the policy to deviate too far from the behavior policy. PPO clips the importance sampling ratio to prevent this. When we update the

entry.title: Q-Learning & Temporal Difference Methods in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: TD learning bootstraps: it updates estimates using other estimates, without waiting for episode completion. The TD error $\delta_t$ is the discrepancy between current and target estimates: $$\delta_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma \max_a Q(S_{t+1}, a) - Q(S_t, A_t)$$ This is the Q-learning TD error. SARSA

entry.title: Autoencoders for Representation Learning | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: An autoencoder learns to compress data into a low-dimensional latent representation and then reconstruct it. The network is forced to learn the most informative features because the bottleneck can’t store everything. import torch import torch.nn as nn class Autoencoder(nn.Module): """Vanilla

entry.title: DBSCAN & Density-Based Clustering in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise) classifies every point as one of three types based on its neighborhood density: flowchart TD A[For each unvisited point] --> B{Has >= min_samples\nneighbors in eps?} B -->|Yes: Core point| C[Start new cluster\nor expand existing]

entry.title: Gaussian Mixture Models & EM Algorithm in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: A Gaussian Mixture Model models the data distribution as a weighted sum of $K$ multivariate Gaussian distributions: $$p(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$ where $\pi_k \geq 0$ are mixing weights with $\sum_k \pi_k = 1$. Unlike K-Means, each point belongs to all

entry.title: K-Means Clustering in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: K-Means partitions $n$ points into $K$ clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squared distances (inertia). Lloyd’s algorithm alternates between two steps until convergence: $$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_k} \|x - \mu_k\|^2$$ flowchart TD A[Initialize K centroids] --> B[Assign each

entry.title: PCA & Dimensionality Reduction in PyTorch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: PCA finds the directions of maximum variance in your data. The first principal component is the axis along which the data is most spread out; the second is orthogonal to the first and captures the next most variance; and so on. The numerically stable way to compute PCA is via Singular Value

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 21: ARM Microarchitecture Deep Dive
entry.summary: Understanding what happens below the ISA boundary explains every counter-intuitive performance result from Part 18. This part maps the journey from instruction fetch and branch prediction down through register renaming, the reorder buffer, execution ports, store queues, and the cache/TLB hierarchy.

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 20: Building a Bare-Metal OS Kernel
entry.summary: Building an OS kernel from scratch on ARM64 is the ultimate integration of everything in this series: boot sequence, exception vectors, memory management, and scheduling all collide in a few hundred lines of assembly and C. This part walks through each layer, from the first instruction after reset

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 19: Reverse Engineering & ARM Binary Analysis
entry.summary: Reverse engineering flips the assembly workflow: instead of writing instructions, you read them to understand what a binary does. ARM's ubiquity across Android, iOS, and embedded firmware makes it the most important architecture for binary analysis today. Mastering ELF layout, symbol conventions,

entry.title: ARM Assembly Part 18: Performance Profiling & Micro-Optimization
entry.summary: Writing fast assembly is half craft, half measurement. ARM's Performance Monitoring Unit gives you hardware-level insight into every stall, cache miss, and mispredicted branch. Combined with Linux perf and a principled micro-benchmarking methodology, you can systematically identify and eliminate

entry.title: Part 1: Why Neural Networks & Their History - Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Neural networks are the engine behind the most transformative technology of the 21st century. They power voice assistants, translate languages in real-time, generate photorealistic images, write code, and diagnose diseases from medical scans. But why did we need them in the first place? Consider

entry.title: Part 2: Building Blocks — Neurons, Weights & Activations - Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: The artificial neuron is the fundamental unit of every neural network. Inspired by biological neurons, it takes multiple inputs, multiplies each by a weight, sums the results, adds a bias, and passes the result through an activation function to produce an output. Think of it as a tiny

entry.title: Part 3: How Neural Networks Learn | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Imagine tuning a radio with dozens of knobs. Each knob position affects the clarity of the signal. Your goal: find the exact combination that minimizes static and maximizes clarity. Neural network training works the same way — except instead of a few knobs, you have millions of “weights” to adjust,

entry.title: Part 4: Build Your First Neural Network from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: In Part 1 of this series, we proved that a single perceptron cannot solve the XOR (exclusive or) problem. A single neuron can only learn linearly separable patterns — and XOR is fundamentally not linearly separable. This limitation, famously highlighted by Minsky and Papert in 1969, nearly killed

entry.title: Part 5: Neural Network Architectures Overview | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Neural networks are not one-size-fits-all. Over the past four decades, researchers have designed specialized architectures to tackle fundamentally different types of data and problems. Each architecture introduces a specific inductive bias — a structural assumption about the data that allows the

entry.title: Part 6: CNNs Deep Dive - Convolutions from Scratch | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Fully connected networks treat every pixel as an independent feature. For a modest 28×28 grayscale image, that means 784 input neurons — and if we connect them to just 128 hidden neurons, we already have over 100,000 parameters in the first layer alone. Scale that to a 224×224 RGB image and the

entry.title: Part 7: RNNs & LSTMs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Feedforward neural networks (FNNs) process each input independently — they have no concept of order or context. When you feed an FNN a word, it has no idea what came before it. But language, time series, audio, and countless other real-world signals are inherently sequential: the meaning of “bank”

entry.title: Part 8: Autoencoders & GANs Deep Dive | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: Unlike supervised learning where we have labels guiding our models, autoencoders learn entirely without labels. The goal is to discover a compressed representation — an encoding — that captures the essential features of the data. This is the foundation of generative modeling: if we can learn a good

entry.title: Part 9: Transformers & Best Practices | Neural Networks Mastery
entry.summary: In 2017, the landmark paper “Attention Is All You Need” by Vaswani et al. fundamentally changed deep learning. The key insight was radical: instead of processing sequences one step at a time (like RNNs and LSTMs), look at ALL positions simultaneously. This single architectural choice unlocked

entry.title: Deep Dive: GANs — Generative Adversarial Networks | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Imagine a counterfeiter who tries to produce fake currency that looks identical to real bills, and a detective whose job is to catch the fakes. As the counterfeiter gets better at forging, the detective must sharpen their skills to tell real from fake. Over time, this adversarial competition pushes

entry.title: Deep Dive: GPT-2 Mini — Building a Language Model | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: A language model is, at its core, a probability distribution over sequences of tokens. Given a sequence of preceding words (or tokens), a language model predicts what comes next. This is the fundamental task behind GPT-2, ChatGPT, and all modern large language models — they learn to predict the

entry.title: Deep Dive: ResNet — Residual Networks from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Before ResNet was introduced in 2015, a perplexing phenomenon haunted the deep learning community: deeper networks performed worse than shallower ones . This wasn't simply overfitting — even the training error was higher for deeper models. A 56-layer plain convolutional network consistently

entry.title: Deep Dive: U-Net — Image Segmentation from Scratch | PyTorch Mastery
entry.summary: Image segmentation is the task of assigning a class label to every single pixel in an image. Unlike classification (which outputs one label for the whole image) or object detection (which draws bounding boxes), segmentation produces a pixel-perfect map that outlines every object's exact boundary.
